title: Monolith to Microservices
summary: Strangler Fig pattern, Branch by Abstraction, data migration strategies, and how to safely decompose a monolith.

sections.content:
## Why Migrate — and Why It's Hard

Monoliths are not inherently bad. They become painful when:
- Deployments are risky (large blast radius)
- Teams step on each other's code
- One module's performance affects the whole app
- You need to scale specific capabilities independently

Migrating to microservices is risky. The **Strangler Fig** pattern is the safest approach — you incrementally replace the monolith rather than doing a big-bang rewrite.

sections.title: Strangler Fig Pattern
sections.content:
graph LR
    subgraph Phase 1 - Intercept
        C[Client] --> P[Proxy/Gateway]
        P -->|all traffic| M[Monolith]
    end
    subgraph Phase 2 - Extract
        C2[Client] --> P2[Proxy/Gateway]
        P2 -->|/orders/*| OS[New Order Service]
        P2 -->|all other| M2[Monolith]
    end
    subgraph Phase 3 - Strangle
        C3[Client] --> P3[Proxy/Gateway]
        P3 -->|/orders/*| OS3[Order Service]
        P3 -->|/payments/*| PS3[Payment Service]
        P3 -->|remaining| M3[Shrinking Monolith]
    end
    style OS fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style M3 fill:#6b7280,color:#fff

## Strangler Fig Step by Step

1. **Insert a proxy** in front of the monolith — all traffic flows through it
2. **Identify** a bounded context to extract (choose one that is relatively self-contained)
3. **Build** the new service alongside the monolith, don't touch existing monolith code
4. **Route** traffic for that capability to the new service via the proxy
5. **Remove** the old code from the monolith (now dead)
6. Repeat for the next capability

### Good first extractions:
- **Leaf services** — few upstream dependencies (email, PDF generation)
- **High-value capabilities** — worth the effort (payment processing)
- **Independent scalability** — image processing, search indexing

sections.title: Data Migration Strategy
sections.content:
graph TD
    Phase1[Phase 1: Shared Database\nNew service reads/writes same DB] --> Phase2
    Phase2[Phase 2: Database per Service\nDual-write period — write to both DBs] --> Phase3
    Phase3[Phase 3: Migration Complete\nNew service owns its DB\nMonolith reads via service API]
    Note1[Easiest — no data migration\nTight coupling still exists]
    Note2[Transitional — sync data\nEventually consistent]
    Note3[Target — fully decoupled]
    Phase1 --- Note1
    Phase2 --- Note2
    Phase3 --- Note3
    style Phase3 fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style Phase1 fill:#dc2626,color:#fff

sections.title: Don't break the database apart prematurely
sections.content: The shared database is a temporary state, not a permanent anti-pattern during migration. It's better to move the API boundary first, then the data. Breaking the DB too early forces you to handle distributed transactions before you have the infrastructure to do it safely.

sections.question: What is the key advantage of the Strangler Fig pattern over a big-bang rewrite?
options: It is faster than a big-bang rewrite
The new system is built incrementally alongside the monolith — you can stop at any point, and the monolith handles traffic until each piece is proven
It avoids the need for a proxy layer
It doesn't require any data migration
sections.explanation: The Strangler Fig incrementally replaces the monolith while it keeps running. At any point, only the extracted capabilities are in the new service — if something goes wrong, you reroute back to the monolith. Big-bang rewrites have a dangerous 'nothing works until everything is done' period and a high probability of never completing.
